author: @LawrenceMarks27
text: Sorry to make this comment unrelated to the post, but I've just finished blasting through a couple hundred of your videos to "catch up" and I've been dying to ask a question.  You stated that pursuit of perfection is not what we're after.  We're after real life solutions to real life problems.  However, you seem to be very headstrong about doing a full test of any of the windmills (to get watts) and that its not the overall point, the point is gaining efficiency.  I was wondering if you'd consider maybe doing a "full build test" quarterly or maybe every 6 months?  That way those of us that are looking to take advantage of your idea's, can potentially latch on to a prototype solution to get started with, while efficiency continues to be pursued with great diligence.  Appreciate the time if you get around to reading this.  You are an inspiration (legitimately).

Perhaps her  required skill set isn’t as high as the old method, but the product is far superior

author: @Patriarchtech
text: Nice vid Robert I totally agree. I have two 3d printers a 300x300 Sapphire CoreXY and and an Original Ender 3 I modified them a bit to get rid of fan noise.(replaced the motherboard on the Ender3 as the original 8bit board was pants) I have the printers because I can tailor make stuff like boxes for electronics, drain grills, gaskets, brackets that I would otherwise have to buy. The other day  the lid on my Nespresso machine snapped so I got out the calipers took measurements and designed a replacement. You probably can't buy a replacement even if you wanted to. Also this means I don't need to buy a new Nespresso and recycle the old one which is good for my pocketbook and for the environment. It is just so handy to have and it has so many applications if you use your imagination as you correctly say. Also this is the perfect tool if you are into "the right to repair" way of thinking which  I am a huge advocate for.  Furthermore I build water turbines and with the printers I can shape casings fittings and plumbing just the way I want it with out having to spend a fortune. Sure you need to augment the printed parts with bearings and couplers etc. but the majority of the construction can be printed. The CAD modelling was a challenge the first week or two, but it is so rewarding once you get a hang of it. Dialing your settings in in the slicer to get the right strength in the parts you print was also a bit tricky but not to bad. I am a very computer savvy guy if I should say so myself which certainly helps, but I don't think the learning curve here is anywhere near as steep as people might think. I mean I am certainly not a spring chicken, and I have zero patience and if I can do it anybody can lol. If I may give you a suggestion: I am not familiar with the export options in Tinkercad, but generally speaking the .stl format is needed for your slicer like CURA or whatever but STL is a not very good format for anything else especially so If you want to import it in to other CAD software. This is because when you render the stl, the model (or the faces on the model) gets converted into triangle shapes and this is an absolute nightmare to work with. It would be extremely helpful if you exported your creations to a common industry standard format like .STEP. This format can be read and imported correctly into almost all CAD software without the triangles. This way people can improve your designs. Anyways just a suggestion keep up the good work. Cheers!

author: @unicornadrian1358
text: A great, thought provoking video, Rob. Technology is a double edged sword, it enables more people to express themselves easily in a given media, but at the same time reduces the skill and knowledge base associated with that medium. It removes the gate keeping associated with many crafts, at the same time as it reduces the learning curve associated with that craft. The issue that it’s a one way ticket that requires ever more complicated tools that users aren’t able to repair or maintain for themselves. A tradesman skilled in old methods and tools can easily add more modern tools to their arsenal. Unfortunate a person who has only experienced modern technology, has little to  no chance of using the tools that came before their time. I’m my industry, we have people who design jewellery on CAD software, then send the files to a casting house to have the piece made. Then the pieces and the appropriate gems get sent to a setter to set the stones and finish the ring. The creator then wants to call themselves a jeweller with none of the skills a jeweller has. This is upsetting to those who have spent years on the tools honing their craft in order to earn the title of jeweller.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thank you mate and I get what you saying. It is a complex issue for sure and i broke it down into a five minute intro so there is a lot to explore there. I think the idea of skill reduction isn't right though - I think the skill base is spread over a larger number so it looks like reduction - for example i could - if I wanted - build a 3D printer from scratch and repair everything on the machine - that's no mean feat and know a lot of people much younger than me who could do the same - so i think there is an element of dis-service there to the young. The idea of a service house has been around a lot longer than 3d printing and CAD but with the growth of interest far more people are trying to 'do it themselves' - that means that working in an industry you see more numbers approaching you but i would argue the proportion hasn't changed you don't see the other side as those folks don't approach you and so it skews a view a little - but - very very interesting topic mate and thank you for bringing it up

author: @totherarf
text:
The interesting bit will be tweaking it!
Scanning something allows you to copy it, but Tweaking it allows you to Evolve an object to compensate existing shortfalls in design!

With Garage Scanners I see a future where you go in with a broken part and you get a new freshly made part that fits. Obviously the car manufacturers will not like that but, if you take a recent example of a Tesla with stone damage to a small plastic part replacement of which at Tesla garage costs over $20K  (seriously) I see people voting with their feet ..... or more precisely their Credit Card ......... (now if they could scan those it could get interesting) ;o)

author: @11Sam11
text: I agree some things on what you've just said like expressing your creative imagination through 3D printing being as a tool. But on the other hand being overly reliant and dependant you'll develop tendency to become complacent and lazy. Real practical engeering is based purely hands on interaction and there are studies that can prove to be the case. A large portion of the brain, a neuro connection links between the brain and hands which plays a integral role for the development of the motor and sensory functioning of the brain. Just twenty-four hours of disuse or immobilization decreases motor cortex excitability and reduces activation in the somatosensory cortex. So overall it's important to gain a habit of balance and not loose your natural intelligence in the process. I should address this matter while organic intelligence is becoming a rarity these days. Btw I also have a 3D printer and only use when machinery parts becomes too expensive but not to worship it daily and forget ourselves what we are truly capable of as grand architects. A true artist of the craft can relate to this and appreciate the natural beauty of human dexterity

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
An associate who specialised in reliability, made the statement "You get the reliability you design into the item!"
My experience says that "You get the maintainability you design into the item!"
3D models can certainly help with the latter.
I saw a 3D printout of a person's pelvis hip joint done from a MRI scan. It offered the surgeon an excellent view of the patient's problem and could also be used to practise the surgery.
For me 3D printing offers the convenience of not having to drive to a machine shop to fetch the part(s)

replies.text:
History has a rose tint. 

Not only was it written primarily at the time by the rich and privileged, like you said it's also analyzed with a tendency to ignore the bad and focus on the good. Which is especially a problem when the good thing was only happening because the bad thing was as well.

replies.author: @Ewr42
replies.text:
​@KaiserTom for a long time history was about the great men, women and their struggles were invisibilized, as well as the masses of servants.

History was about stories, not the actual historical materialism and how it changed with times.


Even more important than what is written in history, is what isn't even mentioned, what's deliberately obfuscated and left out to be forgotten and erased.


I wonder how much of ancient history is locked up in the vatican's libraries and monasteries too. (As well as in caves high up on canyons like some gnostic texts, buried, and hidden in various other ways)

"History" as we know it, was carefully constructed by the academic elite under influence(and threats) of the catholic church, emperors, kings, and other powerful people and institutions eager to maintain their narrative and status quo through cultural mass manipulation.

It isn't about individual psychology, it's millenary social engineering.


The Incas were ten-fold more advanced than the romans ever got to be, but we need to use social darwinism to label them as primitive people to justify their genocide by the spanish conquistadores and to further reinforce eurocentrism.

What about Oriental History? It's almost invariably reduced to mysticism and magical-like thinking.

Most advancements in science and mathematics were made in the middle east, yet europeans appropriated their knowledge and labeled it as their own "discoveries".



We MUST always be critical about the hegemonic narratives we've been fed as propaganda to perpetuate the status quo.

Nothing is unbiased, and history is the most heavily biased area of knowledge since neanderthals were painting caves and creating myths about great warrior heroes.

It's all politics, everything is politics.
Politics is a fundamental part of anything even remotely related to humans, specially the material reality in which we stand on and shapes us and our minds.

author: @TheMadManPlace
text:
Hi Robert,
If you think that you are excited about the possibilities when you look at what a 3D printer can do with what us (lets face it realistically) rather weak material then you are going to be over the moon once you get your hands on a robust 5 axis milling machine where you can make "stuff" out of materials that will be more than up to whatever task you point them at.
And with a CNC milling machine you are not limited to some or other plastic where the finished product has all sorts of built in flaws such as material adhesion issues and lets face it, not much dimensional accuracy etc.
But you will get there I am sure...

text:
I will be following along with your 3D scanning ventures. 
I have not tried 3D scanning myself as it seemed like an expensive investment and the scanners themselves looked hard to use reliably.
I have tried photogrammetry with some free software and a mobile phone camera, that was "good enough" for sculptures, but it was tricky to get good details and it took ages using all my computers resources for hours using COLMAP, Meshroom is faster, but I found COLMAP to be easier to tweak to get a perfect result , but to be honest it was years ago I tried anything like it.

For the whole 3D printer discussion, I guess every man to himself! I am happy that I got into 3D printing, I have made so much creative work that I would never have been able to do with coventional materials. A roll of plastic can become anything I want it to be! Me and especially my dad used to hoard and teardown all kinds of old hardware and scrap and keep it under the bed, just for that special occasion in the future where we needed a 4:1 gear in nylon from some old printer or some other random junk. Now I live a hoarding free life, if I need a gear, I can print a gear. I stock materials that are versatile like threaded rods, boards and sheets of various materials and I still keep steppers and motors when I find them, but thats about it.

author: @rjds1800
text: I love it when you go places, I always get the feeling of a man out of time, in that you have knowledge beyond the norm.

author: @zfid
text: Absolutely agree with your sentiments.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: I think the English government is investigating why the electric usage is going up in your neighborhood. 😅

author: @peterfelecan3639
text: Well, when you'll use 3D scanners you'll see that the workflow is cumbersome and the results mediocre at best, at least with today's cheapo technology. Of course, that changes, as for industrial additive manufacturing, when you use *industrial* scanners. Sometimes, it's just an illusion taken advantage by providers of low quality tools as the printers that you use and promote. The real road toward the usage that you give in the example of the garage mechanics is to have access to good CAD tools and the 3D models, provided by their designers, of the products that you wish to repair or enhance and the good tools that empowers you to create the real word object.

author: @Nanna-hpi
text: Love your way of embracing life.

author: @TheLidlClan
text:
The problem as I see it is why bother with hand crafting at all, Let's all buy a machine to do the work. 
And call it hand made as people do, because they programmed the computer. I cut, edge, tool, dye, leather
don't bother just get a laser cutter. I do understand why you use a 3D printer Robert, but please do not call your creation hand made as people do. The whole thing is destroying skilled crafting.................... Love your channel

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh i make no pretense to hand made mate
